questions.text: Which four object types make up the Git object model?

options.text: file, directory, commit, branch
blob, tree, commit, tag
blob, index, commit, ref
file, snapshot, pointer, tag

questions.explanation: Git's object model has exactly four types: blob (raw file content), tree (directory listing of blobs and sub-trees), commit (snapshot with tree pointer, parent SHAs, author, and message), and tag (annotated tag pointing to any object). Everything stored in .git/objects/ is one of these four types.

questions.text: How does Git determine the identity (address) of an object in the object store?

options.text: Incrementing integer IDs assigned at write time
The filename of the original file on disk
SHA-1 hash of the object's content (including a type/length header)
A UUID generated when the repository is initialized

questions.explanation: Git computes a SHA-1 hash of the object header (type + space + length + null byte) concatenated with the object content. This makes Git a content-addressable store: identical content always produces the same SHA, enabling deduplication, and any tampering changes the SHA.

options.text: The full content of the object
The type of the object (blob, tree, commit, or tag)
The size of the object in bytes
A list of all objects that reference this SHA

questions.explanation: The -t flag makes git cat-file print the type of the object identified by the given SHA. It will output one of: blob, tree, commit, or tag. Use -p to pretty-print the content, and -s to get the size in bytes.

questions.text: Where are loose Git objects stored on disk?

options.text: .git/index
.git/objects/ — first 2 hex chars of SHA = directory, remaining 38 = filename
.git/objects/loose/ — stored flat with full SHA as filename
.git/store/ — one file per commit

questions.explanation: Loose objects live under .git/objects/. Git splits the 40-character SHA-1 hex string: the first 2 characters become the subdirectory name, and the remaining 38 become the filename. So SHA a1b2c3... is stored at .git/objects/a1/b2c3.... Objects are zlib-compressed before storage.

questions.text: What does a Git tree object contain?

options.text: The raw content of all files in a directory
A list of entries with file mode, object type, SHA, and name for each blob or sub-tree
Author, committer, date, and parent SHA of the commit
A binary diff (delta) against the parent tree

questions.explanation: A tree object contains an ordered list of entries. Each entry has: an octal file mode (e.g., 100644 for a regular file, 040000 for a directory), the object type (blob or tree), the SHA-1 of the referenced object, and the entry's name. Trees do not store file content — they just point to blobs and sub-trees.

questions.text: What key improvement does the reftable backend offer over the traditional packed-refs file?

options.text: Stores refs in JSON format for easier debugging
O(log n) lookups via binary search vs O(n) sequential scan, plus atomic updates and a built-in transaction log
Compresses ref names using LZ4 to reduce disk usage
Automatically pushes ref updates to the remote on every commit

questions.explanation: Reftable, production-ready in Git 2.47+, stores refs in a sorted binary table enabling O(log n) binary-search lookups rather than the O(n) sequential scan needed for packed-refs. It also provides atomic multi-ref updates and a built-in transaction log that replaces the separate reflog files.

options.text: Removes untracked files from the working directory
Packs loose objects into packfiles, prunes unreachable objects, and compacts refs into packed-refs
Runs git clean -fd to delete ignored files
Resets the index to match the last commit

questions.explanation: git gc (garbage collection) performs several maintenance tasks: it packs loose objects into packfiles using delta compression, prunes objects that are no longer reachable from any ref (after the configured grace period), consolidates loose refs into packed-refs, and compresses the commit graph for faster traversal.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the `.idx` file that is paired with a `.pack` file?

options.text: It stores the delta-compressed file content that the .pack file indexes
It is a sorted index of SHA-1 hashes with offsets into the .pack file, enabling O(log n) object lookup
It tracks which branches reference objects in the .pack file
It is a human-readable manifest of all commits included in the packfile

questions.explanation: The .idx (index) file contains a sorted list of SHA-1 hashes along with their byte offsets within the corresponding .pack file. This allows Git to quickly locate any object by SHA with a binary search, without scanning the entire pack. The .pack file holds the actual (delta-compressed) object data.

questions.text: What does it mean for Git to be in 'detached HEAD' state, in terms of the `.git/HEAD` file?

options.text: .git/HEAD is empty — no commit is checked out
.git/HEAD contains a raw SHA-1 instead of a symbolic ref like 'ref: refs/heads/main'
.git/HEAD points to a branch that has been deleted
.git/HEAD contains the word 'detached' followed by a branch name

questions.explanation: Normally .git/HEAD holds a symbolic ref: the text 'ref: refs/heads/main' (or whatever branch is checked out). In detached HEAD state — triggered by checking out a specific commit, tag, or remote-tracking ref directly — .git/HEAD holds the raw 40-character SHA-1 of the commit instead. New commits made in this state are not reachable from any branch.

questions.text: What does `git rev-parse HEAD~2` resolve to?

options.text: The SHA of the commit 2 merges before HEAD
The SHA of the commit that is 2 first-parent ancestors before HEAD
The second branch listed in git branch output
The SHA of the second-to-last tag created in the repository

questions.explanation: The tilde (~) syntax traverses first-parent ancestors. HEAD~2 means: start at HEAD, go to its first parent, then go to that commit's first parent — i.e., two hops back along the main lineage. git rev-parse resolves this ancestry expression to the raw SHA-1 of that ancestor commit.
